title: #devex: 1768527751
preview:
Nikhil Sharma: Heads up: we need to update quickstarts/templates to match the new onboarding flow (signup -> API key -> first request -> first 100k tokens). Right now docs + examples are still sending people down the old “pick a model” path and we’re losing folks before first request.

Nikhil Sharma: Target outcome: reduce time-to-first-token. Email + console nudges are pointing to “Hello World” snippet and we should make sure all landing pages/quickstarts match that CTA.

Ben Carter: +1. From email v2 we’re linking to quickstart + “first request” snippet. If quickstart is mismatched, we’re basically A/B testing confusion :grimacing:

Marissa Cole: Also console nudges will be “Create API key” then “Run your first request”. If quickstarts start with “install SDK” but don’t show where to paste the key, we’ll keep seeing stalls.

Dylan Brooks: If you can share the exact copy/CTA you want in quickstart, we can make sure console nudge deep-links align (route + anchors). We’re trying to keep the step names consistent so funnel events aren’t messy.

Nikhil Sharma: Proposed quickstart order:
1) Create API key (link directly to console key page)
2) Set env var
3) Run minimal request (curl + TS + Python)
4) Optional: enable streaming
5) Next: templates/examples

Nikhil Sharma: I’ve started a docs PR to update onboarding docs + links: https://github.com/redwood/redwood-docs/pull/913
Also quickstarts PR with a minimal “first request” snippet: https://github.com/redwood/redwood-quickstarts/pull/412
And an examples template PR (minimal chat app): https://github.com/redwood/redwood-examples/pull/377

GitHub: PR opened: redwood-docs#913 "Update onboarding docs and email links" (author: @nikhil-sharma)
GitHub: PR opened: redwood-quickstarts#412 "Add hello-world activation snippet" (author: @nikhil-sharma)
GitHub: PR opened: redwood-examples#377 "Add minimal chat app template for activation" (author: @nikhil-sharma)

Kenji Nakamura: Quick copy question: are we emphasizing latency or cost in quickstart header? The nudge UI currently says “Get to first token in <2 min” and mentions “low latency” in the subtext.

Ben Carter: For lifecycle touchpoints we’re trying to lead with: fast first token + predictable cost. Reliability as a secondary. “Developer-first” tone.

Ben Carter: Maybe H1: “Make your first request” and a subhead: “Stream responses and see token-level usage in the Console.”

Paula Mendes: Please include instrumentation-friendly wording (don’t promise specific TTFT). We can say “in minutes” but not “<2 min” unless we’re ready to defend it.

Tyler Benson: +1. Also make sure links have UTMs if they’re used in email/retargeting. For docs/quickstarts, we can standardize:
`utm_source=lifecycle&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=quickstart`
and for console deep link:
`utm_source=console&utm_medium=nudge&utm_campaign=activation_checklist`

Nikhil Sharma: Noted. I’ll add UTM guidance in docs PR and keep headers conservative.

Nikhil Sharma: Minimal snippets (WIP) — feedback welcome:

Nikhil Sharma: ```bash
# 1) Set your key
export REDWOOD_API_KEY="<your_api_key>"

# 2) Make your first request
curl https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "redwood/verified-chat",
    "messages": [{"role":"user","content":"Say hello in one sentence."}],
    "stream": true
  }'
```

Dylan Brooks: Looks good. One thing: can we ensure the model name matches whatever the email uses? If email says “redwood/verified-chat” and console default says something else, it’ll look broken.

Marissa Cole: Console nudge copy is currently model-agnostic (“Choose any model from the catalog”). But we do want the snippet to be copy/paste and succeed.

Logan Wright: From telemetry side: if you hardcode a model, please pick one that’s stable and in all regions. We’ve had issues where region X doesn’t have model Y and activation funnels get noisy.

Nikhil Sharma: Good call. I’ll sync with Nadia/Mei on “safe default model” list, but for now can we use whatever the catalog marks as default for hosted? (or a “redwood/verified-*” alias)

Dylan Brooks: We can create/confirm an alias that resolves per-region (this is basically what routing does). But for docs, maybe just say “replace `MODEL_ID` with a model from the catalog” and include one known-good example.

Ben Carter: For activation, we want zero choice points. Folks drop when they have to decide. I’m in favor of one default that “just works” even if it’s not the fastest.

Tyler Benson: Also please make sure templates include the same event names or at least don’t block them. If examples use a different endpoint or an older SDK method, the activation event mapping might miss.

Nikhil Sharma: Re SDK: there’s a TS SDK PR improving missing API key errors (this helps onboarding too): https://github.com/redwood/redwood-sdk-typescript/pull/688
If/when that lands, we should update quickstart to show the nicer error message + link to key creation.

Kenji Nakamura: UX suggestion for docs page: add a small callout after “set env var” like “If you see `401 Unauthorized`, double-check your key and org.” In console we see a lot of confusion there.

Marissa Cole: + add a link to the new email preferences/unsubscribe page in console settings? (separate topic but we’ll likely get asked in docs comments)

Paula Mendes: That link is fine on settings/help pages, but I’d keep the activation quickstart focused. Don’t add extra nav until after first request.

Nikhil Sharma: Plan:
- Update docs PR 913 with step order + conservative copy + UTM guidance
- Update quickstarts PR 412 with snippets (curl/TS/Python), include optional streaming section
- Update examples PR 377 README to point to quickstart and keep template minimal
- Align model ID w/ a stable default (I’ll ask Logan/Marissa offline)

Dylan Brooks: If you want console deep links, the API key page route is stable. DM me the exact URL you want. Also please add anchors on the docs page so email CTA can jump to “Run your first request” section.

Ben Carter: Thanks. Timing: we’d love docs + quickstart updates merged before we ramp email volume (deliverability ramp plan starts end of week).

Nikhil Sharma: Got it. I’ll request reviews today. If anyone sees copy/flow issues, drop comments directly on the PRs.

title: #eng-security: 1768201123
preview:
Samir Patel: FYI CI scan just flagged an AWS access key embedded in the build logs from last night's nightly-run. found it in the artifact cache output. attaching quick grep result.
security-bot: Detection: high-confidence secret match (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID pattern) in workflow run 2026-02-19T03:12Z. SHA: a1b2c3.
Jess Liu: ugh. which repo and job?
Samir Patel: repo: infra/packer-images, job: nightly-build-ami. log path: s3://redwood-ci-artifacts/nightly/2026-02-18/logs/packer.log.gz
Ben Carter: copying grep: ```zcat packer.log.gz | grep -n "AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}" || true``` returned one hit with masked context. looks like a credential output by an errant script that printed `aws configure` debug.
Maria Gomez: immediate actions we should do: 1) revoke the key, 2) rotate any KMS-encrypted assets that used it (if necessary), 3) search other artifacts and commits.
oncall-ryan: I'm oncall, I'll take containment. Can someone tell which IAM user/key id it is?
Samir Patel: key id in the log: AKIAEXAMPLE12345 (masked in log, full value in raw). appears to be associated with user: ci-service-bot (arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/ci-service-bot)
Ben Carter: I'm running account-wide find. command I'm using: ```aws iam list-access-keys --user-name ci-service-bot --query 'AccessKeyMetadata[]'``` and a quick `aws sts get-caller-identity` check from the infra account.
Jess Liu: also run a repo history search to see if the key was ever committed: ```git rev-list --all | xargs -n1 git grep -I --line-number "AKIA" || true```
security-bot: Scan: searching S3 artifact buckets and recent build logs for pattern `AKIA` across all regions. ETA ~5m.
oncall-ryan: Containment done: rotated and disabled the two active access keys for ci-service-bot. Created new key for CI with least-privilege policy. Added temporary deny for older key ids.
Maria Gomez: good. please paste the key IDs and the IAM console link in #incidents once you can. Also create a KMS key rotation task if that key was used to encrypt secrets.
Ben Carter: results: artifact search found same key in 3 other archived logs (older nightly runs). We ran `aws s3api list-objects --bucket redwood-ci-artifacts --prefix nightly/ --query 'Contents[].Key'` then `s3 cp` and grep. No signs of exfil or external copy indicators in S3 access logs so far.
Jess Liu: I'll scrub the artifacts: mark the objects as deleted, replace with redacted copies, and update lifecycle to immediately expire these prefixes for 30 days.
Samir Patel: dev change: the packer recipes were running `aws configure --profile build` from a helper script that had debugging output on failure. fix is to stop printing creds, and add `set -o errexit` and `trap` to hide envs. Opening PR now.
Maria Gomez: post-incident items: add a CI-level secret masking rule, add a pre-commit hook to block `AKIA` patterns, add a step in builds to fail on uncovered secrets (exit code 2), and enable cloudtrail insights for S3 read anomalies.
oncall-ryan: I'll file the incident ticket and mark severity: high (potential secret leak). tagging legal/privacy for review.
Ben Carter: audit: running `git log --all --pretty=format:%H` + `git grep` scan across mirrors. I also ran the internal credential inventory to ensure key had no wide permissions. Policy check: key only allowed S3:GetObject and KMS:Decrypt for specific resources.
Jess Liu: can we add KMS envelope rotation? if the key decrypted anything with customer data we need to escalate. Maria, do you want to own KMS audit?
Maria Gomez: I'll take KMS audit. if we find ciphertext that used that principal, we'll rotate the KMS CMK and re-encrypt. putting that in the incident ticket.
Samir Patel: notify platform users? the service account is used by infra pipelines only; no customer-facing tokens. recommend limited external comms: internal incident note + brief to infra owners.
security-bot: Actions recorded: key revoked, artifacts redacted, PR opened to remove debug prints, CI secret-scan rule added (temporary), incident ticket created: INC-2026-042.
Jess Liu: follow-ups: 1) full retro with timeline, 2) expand CI masking to artifact storage pre-upload, 3) add audit log alert for future `aws configure` prints in logs, 4) training for infra devs. assigning owners in ticket.
Samir Patel: thanks everyone. will post the PR link and the raw log snippet (redacted) to the ticket. planning a 30-min postmortem tomorrow 15:00 UTC.
oncall-ryan: added to calendar. closing slack thread for now, update in ticket when KMS audit completes. :thumbsup:

title: #customer-success: 1767077070
preview:
Asha Rao: FYI - getting multiple reports that EU trial activations are stuck at the "initializing index" step. I exported an affected list (~68 tenants). Need a short customer note + credit policy.

Ben Carter: Infra update: indexing consumers fell behind after a brief downstream 502 storm and one JVM pause on an indexer node. We've capped retries and bumped consumer parallelism at 1767077400. Drain ETA ~35m.

Elena Ruiz: Draft customer copy (short):

Elena Ruiz: "Hi — we're sorry. Some trial accounts in EU may see delayed activation and partial search results while we clear an indexing backlog. Engineers have applied mitigations and we expect normal service within 1 hour.

Impacted trials will receive a $200 credit automatically. If you need expedited handling, book a 1:1: https://cal.redwood.ai/cs-officehours

We'll follow up with an RCA. — Redwood Support"

Asha Rao: Looks good. Standardize credit: $200 for trials <6 months. Manual backfills only for high-touch/renewal-risk accounts. I'll DM the AE list for >$12k ARR.

Ben Carter: Deploy link: https://deploys.redwood.ai/roll/retry-cap-778 — success @1767077605. Queue drain progress: 30% @10m, 72% @30m.

DeployBot: Roll/retry-cap-778 completed — 5 instances updated — 1767077605

Tom Hsu: Quick ops note: don't promise blanket manual backfills in the external message. Offer case-by-case and route to CS booking.

Asha Rao: Agreed. Elena — send first wave to affected trials (in-app + email). I'll prepare a short FAQ: how you'll know it's fixed, credits, and how to request 1:1.

Elena Ruiz: First wave sent to affected list. Subject: "Update — temporary activation delays for some EU trials". Included booking link and credit note.

CS Bot: Incident INC-4981 logged — status: mitigated — affected=68 — office hours link attached.

Ben Carter: Full drain at 1767079860. No data loss detected. We'll add GC tuning and a consumer-lag alert in the postmortem.

Asha Rao: Timeline/checklist: 1) customer email (sent) 2) status page update when drain=100% 3) RCA + tailored renewal-risk comms within 24h 4) credits auto-applied + quick feedback survey within 48h. I'll coordinate AE outreach for flagged accounts.

Elena Ruiz: I'll post engagement metrics and survey link here once credits are applied. :email:

Priya Shah: Quick follow-up from the GPU capacity postmortem. We need clear owners for the autoscale fixes and a tiny validation plan. Keep replies short.
Max Ortega: I can take scheduler tuning and the canary automation.
Ben Carter: I’ll own quota backstop + finance coordination.
Mei Lin: I’ll run replay validation and capture perf before/after.
Rafael Torres: I’ll do the 90-day incident pattern scan and propose two heuristics.
Priya Shah: Top items: 1) quota guardrail + cooldown, 2) scheduler thresholds & smoothing, 3) headroom test harness, 4) detection heuristics.
Max Ortega: Scheduler plan: reduce aggressive scale-down, add 30s smoothing, and set canary at 5% traffic. PR will include revert snippet.
Ben Carter: Quota plan: +10% burst buffer with hard cap and 20m cooldown. Need finance sign-off; drafting request now.
Mei Lin: Validation: staging replay 2h slow-ramp then 30m burst. I’ll post table: qps, p95, gpu_util, cache_hit. ETA Thu.
Rafael Torres: Pattern scan ETA: 4 business days. If I see slow-ramp signature I’ll recommend an incremental headroom multiplier for sessions > 20m.

Ben Carter: Suggested alert: avg(gpu_util) > 87% for 6m -> notify infra + finance. I’ll add `gpu_pool` tag to alerts.
Mei Lin: Will include `gpu_pool` in validation CSV.
Priya Shah: One constraint: all infra changes require a 10m canary and auto rollback if error rate >1% or latency regresses >20%.

title: #sales: 1759999000
preview:
Maya Patel: quick day-0 sync for Merchant PoV. Need to lock agenda, success metrics, and blockers before Monday.
Ethan Liu: propose agenda: stakeholders + access, eval prompts/dataset, staging provisioning, pricing/PO addendum, security Qs.
Sofia Ramos: legal asked about SSO/SCIM and KMS. can we get those on the list?
Ben Carter: buyer wants a 3-week pilot with a token cap instead of a month commit. asked about short-term discount.
Maya Patel: we can do hosted pilot with token cap. Ben, can you draft an addendum with token limits and discount?
Ben Carter: yes, will draft. any target discount?
Ethan Liu: suggest 15-25% for a short pilot; lock model list to one production model. ops note: need CIDR for IP allowlist if requested.
Sofia Ramos: security-bot, questionnaire status?
security-bot: merchant_pov_sq_2026.pdf uploaded to https://redwood.internal/sec/merchant_pov. outstanding fields: data_retention, key_ownership, admin_access_review.
Maya Patel: Sofia, please chase data_retention and key ownership with their security lead.
Sofia Ramos: on it. also confirm whether they require HSM or cloud KMS.
Ben Carter: buyer asked for an eval harness and sample prompts. do we have a template?
Maya Patel: yes: https://redwood.ai/pov-templates. sharing smoke test cURL in thread.
Ethan Liu: I'll provision a staging API key scoped to the pilot and request their CIDR. will share key to merchant-pov@secure-mail by 1759999030.
Maya Patel: proposed success criteria: 1) F1 >= 0.8 on labeled set, 2) 95th pct latency < 250ms for short prompts, 3) successful SSO integration in staging, 4) throughput >= 40 req/s sustained. objections?
Sofia Ramos: add data handling: no raw PII persisted without consent.
Ben Carter: timeline check: kickoff Monday 10am PT. finance signoff on addendum by EOD tomorrow?
Ben Carter: will aim for EOD draft.
Maya Patel: action items: Ben - PO addendum; Sofia - confirm SSO/KMS/HSM and update questionnaire; Ethan - provision staging creds & request CIDR; Maya - send invite + share eval template.
Ethan Liu: also request everyone to drop two prompt examples (classification, extraction, freeform) before EOD to seed the harness.
Maya Patel: thanks team. follow-up after kickoff.

title: #eng-infra: 1755500123
preview:
Maria Gomez: FYI — noticed a ~28% bump in GPU spend for Feb compared to forecast. Most of it shows up as "untagged" in the billing export. Anyone else seeing this?
Kevin Li: can you paste the billing export link? we should run the usual BigQuery grouping.
Maria Gomez: https://console.cloud.google.com/billing/exports — I exported to bigquery this morning. top-line is GPUs in us-central1 and europe-west4 appearing under project:infra-shared.
Sachin Rao: infra-shared is our shared GPU pool for sandbox teams, right? sounds like workloads are spinning up without cost center tags.
Lina Park: yep, some onboarding scripts default to project-level creds and don't set labels. we need enforcement + backfill.
Ben Carter: quick reproduction: my local job uses the shared pool and i didn't get prompted to set label. we should default a label on the compute template.
InfraBot: Deploy 17592 succeeded in infra-shared (autoscaler v3).
Kevin Li: proposed plan: 1) run a backfill query to map untagged charges by resource id -> try to infer team from git repo/owner OR instance naming 2) add a deny policy for create without tag for new instances 3) run a weekly job to alert on >5% untagged GPU spend
Maria Gomez: agree. for (1) can we start with BigQuery and GCP resource labels? here's a query i used last time to surface GPU-hour untagged spend:
```sql
SELECT service.description, sku.description, labels, SUM(cost) as total_cost
FROM `billing_project.gcp_billing_export_v1_v2.gcp_billing_export_v1_*`
WHERE EXTRACT(MONTH FROM usage_start_time)=2 AND sku.description LIKE '%NVIDIA%'
GROUP BY service.description, sku.description, labels
ORDER BY total_cost DESC
LIMIT 200
```
Lina Park: nice. add resource.name to correlate with instance/VM id. also join with metadata table to try owner label inference.
Sachin Rao: for backfill heuristics: instance name prefix -> team mapping, project IAM last modified -> likely owner, and GCS artifact egress logs to map repos. none perfect but should get most.
Abby (Finance): Important — Finance needs a clean mapping for chargeback this quarter. if backfill is noisy we should tag ambiguous as "shared-pool" and bill to central infra budget, not product teams.
Ben Carter: +1. prefer central first then reallocate if teams accept responsibility. prevents surprise invoices.
Maria Gomez: also need to revisit reserved commitments. current committed GPU hours for us-central1 (reserved) aren't aligned to consumption — we're paying for unused reserved capacity in ap-southeast while demand is in eu-west4. Suggest: evaluate convertible/reservable commitments or move some commitment credits to region-flex if provider allows.
Kevin Li: action: run per-region 12-week utilization for GPU SKUs, output table: region, sku, reserved_hours, used_hours, utilization_pct, potential_savings_if_rightsize. i can script it.
Lina Park: note: Dedicated (reserved) pools for Dedicated customers vs shared pools for infra-shared have different billing attachments. don't mix commitments with shared ephemeral pools unless we can partition quotas.
Sachin Rao: cloud provider offers regional discount tiers at 1yr/3yr. we should model cost under current growth: if we commit to 1yr in us-central we save ~22% but could leave us exposed if workloads move. consider convertible reservations or shorter terms for volatile workloads.
Abby (Finance): run two models: conservative (no growth), expected growth (10% qtr over qtr), and aggressive (30%). show NPV and payback period. finance can sign off on a 1-yr commit if payback <9 months.
Maria Gomez: For chargeback mechanics: propose tagging schema: cost_center, product, env, owner, pool. enforce cost_center and owner on any GPU allocation. Also add an automated Slack notify when pool usage spikes > threshold and list top 5 untagged instances.
Kevin Li: we already have a Cloud Function that runs on new instance create — i'll add label enforcement and auto-reject if missing (with a bypass for infra-admin role and an exception request flow).
Ben Carter: for existing VMs: i'll run a backfill job that writes inferred labels to a staging table and creates PRs to owners for confirmation. PRs will be auto-approved after 14 days and then labels applied.
InfraBot: Alert: untagged-gpu-hours > 50 in last 24h (infra-shared).
Maria Gomez: ok timeline: Kevin + Ben do backfill + enforcement this week. Sachin to produce 12-week utilization report by Friday. Abby will run commit financial models by next Wed. Lina will update ops runbook and add exception flow.
Lina Park: raising one constraint — network egress between regions inflates cross-region cost, so moving workloads between regions to match commitments may introduce net-negative savings. account for inter-region egress in modeling.
Sachin Rao: good catch. i'll include estimated egress costs per TB between candidate regions.
Abby (Finance): please add a column for tax & marketplace fees; they vary by region.
Maria Gomez: owners: @Kevin Li (labels + enforcement), @Ben Carter (backfill), @Sachin Rao (utilization + egress modeling), @Abby (financial models), @Lina Park (runbook). ping me if blocked. :thumbsup:
Kevin Li: i'll create the PRs and a small helm chart change for default labels, linking to enforcement policy.
Ben Carter: starting backfill job now, will post results in this thread when the first pass finishes.
Maria Gomez: thanks all, want this done before next billing close.
Ben Carter: first-pass results: 62% of untagged GPU-hours were attributable to 3 project IDs (sandbox-*, experiments-*). leaving the rest as shared-pool. I'll upload csv to gs://infra-ops/chargeback/backfill-1755500123.csv and post a summary in #eng-infra-next-week.
Maria Gomez: perfect, let's review on THU 11:00am.

Kevin Li: action: run per-region 12-week utilization for GPU SKUs, output table: region, sku, reserved_hours, used_hours, utilization_pct, potential_savings_if_rightsize. i can script it.
Lina Park: note: Dedicated (reserved) pools for Dedicated customers vs shared pools for infra-shared have different billing attachments. don't mix commitments with shared ephemeral pools unless we can partition quotas.
Sachin Rao: cloud provider offers regional discount tiers at 1yr/3yr. we should model cost under current growth: if we commit to 1yr in us-central we save ~22% but could leave us exposed if workloads move. consider convertible reservations or shorter terms for volatile workloads.
Abby (Finance): run two models: conservative (no growth), expected growth (10% qtr over qtr), and aggressive (30%). show NPV and payback period. finance can sign off on a 1-yr commit if payback <9 months.
Maria Gomez: For chargeback mechanics: propose tagging schema: cost_center, product, env, owner, pool. enforce cost_center and owner on any GPU allocation. Also add an automated Slack notify when pool usage spikes > threshold and list top 5 untagged instances.
Kevin Li: we already have a Cloud Function that runs on new instance create — i'll add label enforcement and auto-reject if missing (with a bypass for infra-admin role and an exception request flow).
Ben Carter: for existing VMs: i'll run a backfill job that writes inferred labels to a staging table and creates PRs to owners for confirmation. PRs will be auto-approved after 14 days and then labels applied.
InfraBot: Alert: untagged-gpu-hours > 50 in last 24h (infra-shared).
Maria Gomez: ok timeline: Kevin + Ben do backfill + enforcement this week. Sachin to produce 12-week utilization report by Friday. Abby will run commit financial models by next Wed. Lina will update ops runbook and add exception flow.
Lina Park: raising one constraint — network egress between regions inflates cross-region cost, so moving workloads between regions to match commitments may introduce net-negative savings. account for inter-region egress in modeling.
Sachin Rao: good catch. i'll include estimated egress costs per TB between candidate regions.
Abby (Finance): please add a column for tax & marketplace fees; they vary by region.
Maria Gomez: owners: @Kevin Li (labels + enforcement), @Ben Carter (backfill), @Sachin Rao (utilization + egress modeling), @Abby (financial models), @Lina Park (runbook). ping me if blocked. :thumbsup:
Kevin Li: i'll create the PRs and a small helm chart change for default labels, linking to enforcement policy.
Ben Carter: starting backfill job now, will post results in this thread when the first pass finishes.
Maria Gomez: thanks all, want this done before next billing close.
Ben Carter: first-pass results: 62% of untagged GPU-hours were attributable to 3 project IDs (sandbox-*, experiments-*). leaving the rest as shared-pool. I'll upload csv to gs://infra-ops/chargeback/backfill-1755500123.csv and post a summary in #eng-infra-next-week.
Maria Gomez: perfect, let's review on THU 11:00am.

title: #eng-platform: 1755004327
preview:
Alex Kim: Quick follow-up to the RFC — proposal to move 'circuit-breaker' decision from router to observer service and make router do only routing+fallback execution. Thoughts?

Priya Singh: curious — what's the motivation? operationally it's easier to centralize decisions, but we lose local fast-fail ability.

Alex Kim: Main reasons: 1) observer has aggregated cross-model signals (error-rate spikes, latency p50/p95, token-queue-growth) to make more informed degrade decisions; 2) easier to share safety policies across hosted/dedicated/private. Router still needs a fast local check (cacheable) for immediate O(1) rejects.

Ben Carter: Do we have an API surface sketched? e.g., router calls observer.isHealthy(model, tenant, cohort) sync -> bool? That could add latency.

Jia Li: maybe make it async: router consults cached verdicts (ttl 5s) + probe hook to observer for stale entries. If cache miss, fallback to local heuristics and mark for async reconciliation.

Alex Kim: exactly. Example config snippet:
```
fallback_policy:
  decision_owner: observer
  cache_ttl_ms: 5000
  local_heuristic_thresholds:
    latency_ms: 1200
    error_rate_pct: 5
```

Priya Singh: love the cache TTL idea. we should add a 'grace_count' for in-flight requests so we don't cut mid-flight.

Ben Carter: how do we signal rollbacks for canary cohorts? want automatic fallback when cohort error delta > X and sustained > Y secs.

Jia Li: propose signals: delta_error_pct, delta_latency_ms, unusual_token_stop_rate. algorithm: streaming EWMA + burst detector. trigger if EWMA(delta_error) > 3% && sustained 60s.

deploy-bot: deploy started: canary-control-plane v2 -> staging (sha: abc123) :rocket:

Alex Kim: ops note: router should allow a 'soft-fallback' (route to old variant but continue collecting shadow telemetry) vs 'hard-fallback' (stop routing to variant). that way we can revert without losing signal.

Priya Singh: also need a cooldown/backoff: if observer flips fallback state repeatedly, escalate to manual and open incident.

Ben Carter: can we keep a per-tenant risk-budget? small tenants shouldn't trigger system-wide fallbacks.

Jia Li: yes, decision_owner should take tenant quota into account. Include weight-attenuation. config example continues: 
```
cohort_rules:
  - name: small-tenant
    weight_threshold: 0.01
    escalate_to_manual: true
```

Alex Kim: next steps: I'll draft an RFC appendix with API schemas, cache semantics, and a proposed EWMA formula. Please comment on the heuristics above by EOD.

Priya Singh: ack

Ben Carter: ack

Jia Li: will add sample probe graphs

Alex Kim: thanks all :thumbsup:

title: #docs: 1751239999
preview:
Amy Walker: Quick heads-up: community reported broken notebook snippets after v2 docs sync. Need urgent patch. Details: examples/notebooks/chat_example.ipynb failing to run due to 'Responses.create' change.
Ben Carter: I'll take notebook fixes. Can someone confirm runtime token var name?
Jill Moreno: v2 uses RW_TOKEN. Notebook still references RW_API_KEY. I can PR the change and add a small note.
Pedro Ruiz: Also saw issues with model alias mapping in sample apps (legacy -> redwood-chat). Opening issue: https://github.com/redwood-inference/docs/issues/789
Amy Walker: Thx. Also legal wants 'sunset' language eyes — @Legal please confirm.
Legal: Confirmed, use "Deprecated endpoints will be removed after 2025-06-01; calls will receive HTTP 410 with guidance to /docs/migration" in the public notice.
Ben Carter: PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/docs/pull/624 — includes notebook update + test cell that runs a sample call. Added 'try/except' to fail gracefully.
Samira Khan: I'll add a small compatibility shim in examples/compat/shim.js to translate v1 streaming NDJSON to v2 framed events. Snippet:
```js
// simple shim outline
const http = require('http')
http.createServer((req, res) => {
  // proxy to v2, reframe events into legacy ndjson
})
```
Evan Li: Can we add a CI job that runs notebooks (nbconvert execute) and smoke tests against the staging docs? I'll add to .github/workflows/docs-smoke.yml
Amy Walker: Yes please. Make job conditional on label 'deprecation-hotfix' so it runs for these PRs. Docs-Bot: FYI — please add label 'deprecation' and attach smoke output to PR. :robot_face:
Jill Moreno: I'll also add a short user-facing FAQ entry: "Why did my API key header stop working?" with example curl diff.
Pedro Ruiz: Also, community Slack had a config question: how to migrate embeddings to new dim. I added a small script (pad/trim) and linked it in FAQ.
Evan Li: Drafted workflow: run nb tests -> run smoke curl tests -> run snippet-lint -> post artifact. If all pass, merge and deploy.
Amy Walker: Schedule: hotfix PR today, smoke run tonight, merge tomorrow morning. If any failure, hold announcement.
Docs-Bot: Meeting reminder: #docs-hotfix today 16:00 PT.
Ben Carter: Pushed fix. cc @Community-Team to prepare customer comms.
Community-Team: We'll prepare short notice for affected users and include migration snippet and rollback instructions.
Pedro Ruiz: Note: add redirect for /docs/notebooks/chat_example -> /docs/migration/notebooks/chat_example_v2 and include anchor map.
Amy Walker: Great. Assigning owners: notebooks=Ben, snippets=Jill, shim=Samira, CI=Evan, redirects=Pedro. Thanks team. :tada:

title: #marketing: 1749528123
preview:
Ben Carter: Launch day KPI thread. Dropping live numbers + quick asks here so we don’t lose them across DMs.

Ben Carter: Dashboard (internal): https://mode.redwoodinference.com/benchmark-launch-day (refresh ~2 min). If anything looks off, tag @Tyler Benson.

Tyler Benson: Pulled initial read (last 60 min):
- Landing page sessions: 18.4k
- Unique visitors: 15.1k
- Avg time on page: 2:12
- PDF downloads: 2.9k
- Methodology clicks: 4.1k
- CTA clicks (Try Hosted API): 1.3k
- CTA clicks (Request demo): 212

Tyler Benson: Early funnel:
- Hosted signup starts attributed to /benchmarks: 486
- Completed signups (email verified): 311
- Demo form submits attributed: 64

Soojin Lee: Nice. What are top referrers right now?

Tyler Benson: Top sources (sessions, last 60m):
1) LinkedIn (Camila + company page posts): 6.2k
2) X/Twitter: 4.9k
3) Direct: 3.1k
4) HN: 2.0k (starting to ramp)
5) Newsletter partner (UTM=partner_june): 1.1k

Camila Reyes: HN ramp means we’re about to get “what did you measure / is this cherry-picked” comments. Make sure we’re linking methodology aggressively.

Ethan Wallace: Seeing exactly that on X replies. I’m responding with methodology link + “defined workload mix, see details” and not debating.

Ben Carter: Quick question for @Logan Wright — are we seeing filter interaction events coming through? Want to know if people are actually using the interactive table.

Logan Wright: Yep. Last 30m:
- benchmark_filter_change: 9.7k
- benchmark_metric_toggle: 5.4k
- benchmark_export_click (csv/json): 612
- benchmark_cta_click: 1.6k

Logan Wright: One note: mobile Safari is undercounting ~8-10% due to tracking prevention (expected). Overall trends are solid.

Alicia Perez: Watching Hotjar: a bunch of users are hovering “Latency/$” and then scrolling to find definition. I think we need a one-line microcopy directly under the metric toggle.

Wei Chen: Agree. Current tooltip might be too hidden. Suggest add under toggles:
“Latency/$ combines p50/p95 latency and $/token for a defined production workload. Details in methodology.”

Soojin Lee: +1. Keep it tight + non-claimy. @Wei Chen can you patch copy today?

Wei Chen: Yes. I can land a copy-only PR within the hour if @Felix Schneider confirms where it should sit (no layout break).

Felix Schneider: I can slot it under the metric toggles, one sentence, wraps on mobile. If it’s copy-only we can ship w/out redeploying the results service.

Ben Carter: Do it. This is the #1 confusion point.

Tyler Benson: Heads-up: conversion differs a lot by source.
- LinkedIn: higher PDF downloads, lower Hosted signup starts (more enterprise vibes)
- HN: lower PDF, higher methodology clicks + Hosted CTA

Grace O'Connor: From HubSpot side: “Request demo” form is converting better when people land on methodology first (weirdly). Might be folks doing diligence.

Markus Klein: Makes sense. Also getting inbound asking “can you share the raw data / manifests.” For now I’m sending methodology + offering PoC replication notes.

Soojin Lee: Any signs of the page slowing down / errors? I saw one internal ping about a spinner.

Sean Gallagher: SRE view: marketing site p95 is stable (CDN is doing its job). But the benchmark JSON endpoint had a brief cache miss spike ~10 min ago. No sustained 5xx.

Sean Gallagher: If anyone sees a white screen / table not loading, please grab HAR or at least browser + region.

Ben Carter: Noted. We’ll keep an eye.

Tyler Benson: Quick numbers update (last 2h):
- Sessions: 41.8k
- PDF downloads: 6.7k
- Hosted signup starts: 1,102
- Completed signups: 721
- Demo submits: 141

Camila Reyes: Are we happy with the above-fold CTA labels? “Try Hosted API” might be too product-y for people who just want a takeaway.

Alicia Perez: Agree. Users on enterprise paths are bouncing after reading 1–2 paragraphs. Maybe swap secondary CTA to “Talk to an expert (Dedicated/Private)” instead of “Request demo”?

Soojin Lee: Careful: “Talk to an expert” is fine, but keep it consistent with forms + Sales routing.

Grace O'Connor: If we rename, please keep the underlying UTM/CTA event name the same or we’ll splinter attribution. Label change ok.

Wei Chen: I can change button label only:
Primary stays “Try Hosted API”
Secondary becomes “Talk to Sales (Dedicated/Private)” (same link/track)

Ben Carter: Let’s do it. @Wei Chen ship with the Latency/$ microcopy in the same PR.

Wei Chen: On it.

Logan Wright: FYI if you want clean before/after, post the deploy time here and I’ll annotate in the dashboard so we can compare CTA click-through.

Felix Schneider: If it’s doc-site copy, deploy window is ~10–15 min after merge.

Tyler Benson: Another observation: methodology page has a 78% scroll depth to the “Caching/batching policy” section. That section is doing work.

Ethan Wallace: That’s good; it’s also where critics are going first. Please make sure the caching language stays exactly as Legal approved.

Soojin Lee: @Ethan Wallace / @Camila Reyes any other “quick fixes” to prioritize today?

Camila Reyes: Two asks:
1) Add a short line near the top: “Results vary by workload; we publish full configs + caveats.”
2) If we can, add a “How to replicate” link (even if it goes to FAQ/internal? Actually needs to be public-safe).

Wei Chen: For (2) we can link to the public methodology section “Reproducibility artifacts” (already there) with anchor text “How to replicate.” No new content needed.

Ben Carter: Perfect.

Tyler Benson: Live update (HN just spiked): sessions/min went from ~240 to ~540. PDF downloads/min didn’t move much; methodology clicks/min doubled.

Markus Klein: Sales note: AEs pinging me asking for “one sentence summary” they can paste in outbound today.

Soojin Lee: Use: “We benchmarked production-style LLM inference across regions using a published workload mix, and report latency and cost together (latency/$) plus p50/p95 and $/1M tokens.”

Ethan Wallace: + add link. Please don’t paste screenshots.

Felix Schneider: Copy PR merged. Deploy ETA 12 min.

Logan Wright: Marking deploy timestamp: 1749532018 (approx). I’ll watch CTA CTR + metric toggle interactions for the next hour.

Tyler Benson: Post-deploy (first 10 min):
- Metric tooltip hover rate down (good)
- Methodology clicks steady
- Primary CTA CTR unchanged so far (too early)

Camila Reyes: Keep posting updates here through EOD. This thread is our source of truth for “what happened today.”

Ben Carter: Will do. Next update in ~30 min unless something breaks.

title: #general: 1749520010
preview:
Camila Reyes: It’s live! Benchmark report + interactive page are out in the world 🚀
Links:
• Landing page: https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar
• PDF: https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar/report.pdf
• Methodology: https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar/methodology
Huge thanks to Applied ML + Runtime + Infra + Docs + PMM + Design + Sales Enablement. Drop early feedback / weirdness you see here.

Jordan Blake: Congrats team. This is a big “we can execute cross-functionally” moment. :clap: Please keep an eye out for any community replies/questions we should respond to quickly.

Soojin Lee: +1 and THANK YOU everyone. If you see anyone asking “how did you measure?” please point them to the methodology link above and ping me for anything that needs an official response.

Ethan Wallace: Seeing folks already sharing screenshots of the tables on X. If you spot any confusing chart/tooltips or phrasing, paste the link + quote in-thread and we’ll triage.

Ben Carter: Early funnel numbers (first ~15 min): landing page sessions tracking above forecast. CTA clicks are healthy. Export clicks higher than expected (good sign). Will share a snapshot in #marketing once dashboards settle.

Logan Wright: Confirming analytics events are firing:
- `benchmark_filter_change`
- `benchmark_metric_toggle`
- `benchmark_export_click`
- `benchmark_cta_click`
If anyone sees missing attribution UTM params, send me the URL.

Felix Schneider: Watching the interactive table performance. We’re getting a lot of “filter spam” (people toggling model/region rapidly). Should be fine but if you see slow loads, tell me which browser + region.

Alicia Perez: Design note: mobile is always the risky one for dense tables. If you can, test on your phone and let me know if anything looks broken (especially the disclaimers + tooltips).

Wei Chen: Docs side: links are green on CI and on live. If anyone sees 404s or weird redirects, paste them here.

Jonas Weber: Applied ML: data + methodology are final as published. If you see anyone challenging fairness, we can point to:
- workload definition
- prompt set v3 production mix
- repeated runs + stability gates
We should avoid debating individual point estimates in replies; emphasize distributions/variance + reproducibility.

Claire Dubois: +1. Also reminder: our “latency/$” is normalized; the PDF footnotes explain it. If you see confusion around “why not tokens/sec”, we have an approved answer.

Markus Klein: SE field note: already got 2 inbound pings asking “can I reproduce this in my PoC?” I’m sending the short version: use our harness + same prompt set + same concurrency + caching policy, and expect some regional variance.

Stephanie Nguyen: Got a Slack from an AE: prospect loved the “p95 heatmap” but asked if Dedicated changes the numbers. I told them: same runtime, different capacity isolation + more predictable queueing. Anything else we want to standardize there?

Mateo Alvarez: That’s correct. For public messaging: don’t promise “better latency” on Dedicated; promise predictability/consistency and capacity guarantees. Runtime path is the same.

Lukas Meyer: EMEA folks asking about EU region wording (data residency). The page says “EU regions available” but doesn’t over-specify. Looks good. If someone asks, we should stick to “available regions listed in the filter; talk to sales for residency requirements.”

Naomi Feldman: Quick security scan: public artifacts look clean (no request IDs, no internal cluster names). If you see someone screenshotting raw JSON endpoints, please send me the URL so we can confirm redaction.

Vanessa Ortiz: Infra: traffic is spiky but within reserved headroom. Biggest variable is cache behavior on first load. We’re watching EU + APAC edges.

Sean Gallagher: FYI we saw a brief CDN cache-miss burst right after announcement went out (more origin hits than expected). Not customer-impacting so far. If anyone hears “page slow” from external folks, capture rough timestamp + location.

Neha Kapoor: On-call is staffed for the next few hours. If you see any 5xx/429 chatter, please paste details (screenshots welcome) and we’ll correlate.

inc-bot: Status update: Monitoring alert "Marketing site origin RPS" triggered (warning). Current: 1,820 rps. Threshold: 1,500 rps. Investigating.

Tyler Benson: lol at our own benchmark page getting benchmarked. :)

Ben Carter: Seeing a few reports of “took ~5–8 seconds to load table” from folks in EU. Not consistent. Might be first-load + cache warming.

Felix Schneider: That aligns with what I’m seeing: first paint is fine, but the table data fetch is slower on cold cache. We have a short TTL right now; can bump if needed.

Sean Gallagher: We can tune cache TTL without changing content. If Marketing is okay with slightly less “instant refresh” for the day, we can reduce origin pressure.

Soojin Lee: Yes, please optimize for reliability today. If we need to refresh numbers later, we’ll plan it.

inc-bot: Status update: Mitigation applied: increased CDN cache TTL for benchmark JSON responses. Origin RPS trending down. No incident declared.

Jasmine Liu: CS heads up: Support queue has 3 tickets asking “are these numbers w/ caching?” We’re replying with the methodology link + a short explanation. If we want a one-liner snippet for frontline, send it to me.

Elliot Price: Suggested snippet (approved language from methodology summary): “Results include Redwood’s default production serving settings for this workload, with caching/batching configured as specified in the methodology. We publish the exact configs and rerun thresholds so teams can reproduce and compare.”

Camila Reyes: This thread is exactly why I love this company. Thank you all. Keep dropping feedback + any external questions you see.

Jordan Blake: +1. And remember: be humble in public replies. Lead with methodology + caveats, not victory laps.

Ethan Wallace: Noted. Also, if you see any typos/unclear phrasing, drop a screenshot + location (page section / PDF page #) and we’ll patch.

Alicia Perez: Someone DM’d me: on iPhone Safari the sticky header overlaps the “Disclaimers” accordion when expanded. I’m looking. If anyone can reproduce, please confirm.

Wei Chen: Confirmed on my iPhone 14 / iOS 18.2. It’s minor but real. I’ll file a quick ticket + propose CSS fix.

Felix Schneider: I can ship a quick CSS adjustment today. Ping me the ticket link.

Ben Carter: Last note for now: traffic is still climbing. Biggest source is organic/social, then newsletter. If you’re posting, please use UTM links so we can attribute.

Camila Reyes: Alright, go enjoy the moment for a minute. Then back to watching the dashboards 🙂

title: #marketing: 1749229107
preview:
Soojin Lee: Dropping landing page copy v1 here for review. Please do inline edits + flag anything that needs clearer disclaimers before we route to Legal. Link: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/drafts/benchmarks/landing-page-v1 (internal)

Soojin Lee: Main question: does "latency/$" read clearly enough to devs? Also are we covered on comparative language?

Ethan Wallace: Read-through notes:
- Hero feels strong but a bit absolute.
- The "#1" phrasing in 2 places is going to trigger Legal.
- Disclaimers need to be above the fold *somewhere* (or at least in the table header + persistent tooltip).

Ethan Wallace: Inline edit suggestion for hero:
Current: "The best production inference economics: lowest latency per dollar across top open models."
Proposed: "Production inference economics, measured: latency per dollar across a curated set of open models and regions."

Wei Chen: +1. Also "lowest latency per dollar" is a claim without a scope. If we keep it, we need to say "in our tests" and be explicit about the workload. 

Wei Chen: Copy nit: "latency/$" is jargon-y. In first mention, spell out "latency per dollar (latency/$)" and add a hover definition.

Alicia Perez: From UX side, I can add a small info icon next to the metric label and keep a sticky disclaimer row above the table.

Alicia Perez: Something like:
"Benchmark results are based on a defined workload and may vary by prompt, traffic shape, caching, and model version." (plus link)

Ben Carter: Re CTA block: we should split for PLG vs enterprise.
- Primary CTA: "Try the API" (self-serve)
- Secondary: "Talk to us about Dedicated/Private" (form)
Also please avoid "guaranteed" anywhere. I saw "guaranteed savings" in the benefits bullets.

Ethan Wallace: Yep, I’ll strike that.
Inline: change "Guaranteed lower costs" -> "Lower unit cost opportunities" / "Cost controls to optimize unit economics".

Henry Cho: Product note: we should avoid implying every region/model combo is GA. Add "availability varies by region" and a short callout about "supported vs experimental" models (esp if we show anything that’s not in catalog yet).

Soojin Lee: Good catch. We can add a line under the model filter: "Model availability and support tier vary by deployment mode."

Renee Sullivan: DevEx lens: disclaimer language needs to be readable, not just legal.
Maybe add a "How to read this" section before the table:
1) Pick a region
2) Pick a model
3) Choose context length / concurrency
4) Use p95 + $/1M tokens to sanity-check

Wei Chen: +1 and we should define p50/p95 in plain English. Tooltip copy suggestion:
"p95 = 95% of requests are faster than this latency under the benchmark workload."

Michael Grant: Jumping in with Legal hats. Anything comparative needs:
- Clear scope (which models/regions, dates)
- "In our testing" language
- Link to methodology and raw artifacts *we retain*
- No "best", "#1", "leading", "fastest" unless substantiated and scoped

Michael Grant: Also add: "Benchmarks are not a guarantee of performance in production" and "Results may vary based on workload, settings, and infrastructure." This should be visible on the page (not buried in a PDF only).

Sofia Mendes: Specific phrases to avoid:
- "outperforms" (unless we’re explicitly naming competitor baselines + caveats)
- "cheaper than" / "half the cost" (unless exactly supported)
- "industry standard" (unless we cite a standard)

Sofia Mendes: Safer alternatives:
- "measured"
- "observed"
- "in a defined benchmark"
- "for the tested configuration"

Ethan Wallace: Noted. I’ll remove:
- "Redwood is the fastest way to run X"
Replace with:
- "Redwood is built to optimize latency and cost across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments."

Soojin Lee: For the comparison section, we currently have:
"Redwood delivers 1.8x better latency/$ than alternatives."
We should probably change to:
"Use latency/$ to compare configurations; where we show contextual baselines, see notes and caveats."?

Michael Grant: Yes. And if we show any external baseline: disclose source + conditions + date, and make it clear it’s not an endorsement/affiliation. If we’re not ready, omit competitor numbers from landing page entirely.

Ben Carter: From conversion standpoint I’m fine removing competitor callouts from the landing page. Keep it in the PDF/blog where we can add nuance.

Wei Chen: Agree. Landing page should be about *our* benchmark + methodology credibility.

Alicia Perez: Layout suggestion:
- Hero
- “What we measured” summary (3 bullets)
- Table
- Methodology + FAQs accordion
- CTAs
If we put disclaimers inside the accordion only, people won’t see them.

Renee Sullivan: Can we add an example disclaimer that’s human?
"These numbers reflect one production-like workload (mix of prefill/decode) and are meant to help you estimate trade-offs—not predict your exact app." 

Sofia Mendes: That’s good. Keep it factual.

Ethan Wallace: Pulling actual draft lines that need edits:
1) "Proven best-in-class inference economics" -> "Benchmarked inference economics"
2) "Save 30–60%" -> remove unless we can tie to specific configurations + confidence intervals
3) "Consistent everywhere" -> "Designed for consistent performance across regions; benchmark includes NA/EU/APAC where available"

Henry Cho: Also be careful with "everywhere" because we don’t have every cloud region. 

Soojin Lee: Action items:
- Ethan/Wei: revise hero + benefits + remove absolute claims
- Alicia: add table-level disclaimer + tooltip definitions + placement proposal
- Legal (Michael/Sofia): send exact disclaimer block you want visible on page
- Henry: confirm supported/experimental model list wording

Michael Grant: Proposed disclaimer block (draft, ok to simplify but keep substance):
"Benchmark results are based on Redwood Inference’s testing of a defined workload and configuration as of July 2025. Results may vary based on model version, prompt distribution, concurrency, caching/batching settings, regional capacity, and other factors. Benchmarks are provided for informational purposes and are not a guarantee of production performance. See methodology for details."

Wei Chen: I’ll adapt for readability but keep the elements.

Ben Carter: Please also add a short line near export/download:
"Downloaded data is provided as-is".

Soojin Lee: :thumbsup: thanks all. Please drop your suggested final language in-thread by EOD so we can update v1 and reroute tomorrow.

Ethan Wallace: Will do. I’ll push an updated doc tonight and tag @Soojin @Wei for final pass.

Alicia Perez: I’ll mock the sticky disclaimer row + info tooltips and share screenshots.

Sofia Mendes: Once copy is updated, ping me for a quick claims scan before it goes wider.

Soojin Lee: Done. (And yes, we’re killing "#1" everywhere.)

title: #marketing: 1749067742
preview:
Soojin Lee: Quick naming q for the benchmark page + PDF. We keep saying “latency/$” internally. Do we want to ship that as the public label? Or something less weird / more legally safe?

Ethan Wallace: +1 it reads a little hacker-y. Also ppl will ask “is lower better?” and “whose $?”

Ben Carter: From growth POV, “latency per dollar” is super sticky but I worry it’ll get misquoted as “Redwood is cheaper” vs “more cost-efficient for a given latency.”

Nadia Rahman: Technically the metric is `p50_latency_ms / usd` (and also we’ll show p95 separately). Lower is better. It’s basically “milliseconds per USD for a fixed workload definition.”

Henry Cho: Another concern: “latency/$” sounds like a universal truth, but it’s a composite that depends on our pricing + the workload mix. We need the label to signal that.

Jonas Weber: Agree. If we do keep it, we need the tooltip to define it + the methodology link right there.

Wei Chen: Copy idea: call it “Latency per $ (ms/USD)” and add “lower is better” next to it. Or “Cost efficiency (latency per $)” with a learn-more.

Camila Reyes: My gut: we can’t lead with a symbol. “Latency per dollar” is clearer. But we should also test “Performance per dollar” (more common phrase) even if not as precise.

Soojin Lee: Okay, options I’m hearing:
- “Latency/$”
- “Latency per dollar (ms/USD)”
- “Performance per dollar”
- “Cost efficiency”
Anything else?

Ethan Wallace: “Latency-per-dollar” (hyphenated) reads more like a defined term vs a math expression.

Ben Carter: Also “Latency/$” is going to look like a pricing gimmick to some folks on X. “Performance per $” is a known trope.

Nadia Rahman: “Performance per $” is ambiguous though—what performance? tokens/sec? latency? throughput? We’ll still need to explain.

Soojin Lee: Looping Legal before we lock. @Michael @Sofia can you sanity check what’s safest?

Michael Grant: Skimming. Biggest legal risks:
1) People inferring comparative pricing claims (“cheaper”, “lowest cost”, etc.) without full context.
2) Confusion over what “$” represents (your list price vs customer negotiated pricing vs cloud pass-through).
3) “Per dollar” phrasing could be interpreted as a promise of savings.

Sofia Mendes: +1. Also the symbol “$” in a headline can look like marketing-y “value” claims. Not prohibited, but you need guardrails.

Michael Grant: If you use it, define it immediately as a *computed metric* for a *specific workload* under *published assumptions*. Also avoid absolutes (“best”, “#1”, “industry-leading”) unless you’re ready to substantiate across the full market.

Ethan Wallace: Got it. What about “Latency per $ (Redwood pricing)” as a parenthetical?

Sofia Mendes: That helps, but don’t bury it. I’d prefer: “Latency per dollar (ms/USD) — based on Redwood pricing as of <date>” in a footnote + methodology section.

Ben Carter: Footnote is fine in PDF. On the web table we can do a tooltip with “Based on public Redwood API pricing as of July 2025” etc.

Wei Chen: UX-wise: column header could be “Latency per $” and then an info icon. Tooltip: “Computed as p50 end-to-end latency (ms) divided by estimated USD cost for this workload. Lower is better. See methodology.”

Nadia Rahman: + please don’t call it “value index” lol. That sounds like we made it up to win.

Jonas Weber: Also call out that it’s end-to-end latency including queueing under stated concurrency, not just model kernel time. People will try to compare it to their single-request numbers.

Henry Cho: We should avoid “cost efficiency” alone. It’s too broad and invites “you’re saying you’re the cheapest.” If we keep it, it should always be paired with the actual definition.

Camila Reyes: So we can keep the hook but make it defensible:
- Primary label: “Latency per dollar (ms/USD)”
- Short label in charts: “Latency/$ (lower is better)”
- Always include methodology link + pricing date
- Avoid “cheapest” language

Soojin Lee: This feels right. Question: is “per dollar” safer than the “$” symbol?

Sofia Mendes: Slightly. “Per dollar” reads more like a defined unit; “$” reads like a marketing claim. Either is ok if the definition is right next to it.

Michael Grant: Agree. In headlines / hero copy, prefer words over symbols. In tables, symbol is okay with tooltip + footnote.

Ethan Wallace: For the blog headline, what about “latency-per-dollar benchmark” (as a term) and first sentence defines it.

Ben Carter: Works. Also we should add one sentence: “This is not a statement about your total bill; your results vary by prompt mix, context length, caching, and region.”

Nadia Rahman: One more nuance: if we show competitor baselines at all, we can’t imply we know their effective $ unless we specify assumptions. That’s a separate landmine.

Soojin Lee: Yep. We’ll keep competitor context in “contextual baselines” with heavy caveats (and Legal review). This naming decision is mainly for the Redwood numbers.

Wei Chen: Action items:
- I’ll update web spec copy: “Latency per dollar (ms/USD)” + tooltip text + “lower is better” microcopy.
- Ethan: update report glossary entry for “latency per dollar”.

Soojin Lee: I’ll write the approved phrasing + disallowed phrases for the message house.

Michael Grant: Disallowed suggestions (not exhaustive): “cheapest,” “lowest cost,” “save X%,” “best value,” “#1 performance per $,” “guaranteed savings.”

Sofia Mendes: Approved-ish framing: “cost-efficient inference,” “improved latency-per-dollar,” “based on measured workloads,” “results vary.”

Camila Reyes: Perfect. Let’s lock on:
- Hero/headers: “Latency per dollar”
- Chart/table shorthand: “Latency/$” + info tooltip
(reactions: :thumbsup: x6)

Soojin Lee: Locked. I’ll post the exact wording in the enablement docs once Wei/Ethan updates land.

Ben Carter: pls send me the exact tooltip text too so we don’t drift in emails.

Soojin Lee: will do.
text:
Soojin Lee: Quick naming q for the benchmark page + PDF. We keep saying “latency/$” internally. Do we want to ship that as the public label? Or something less weird / more legally safe?

Ethan Wallace: +1 it reads a little hacker-y. Also ppl will ask “is lower better?” and “whose $?”

Ben Carter: From growth POV, “latency per dollar” is super sticky but I worry it’ll get misquoted as “Redwood is cheaper” vs “more cost-efficient for a given latency.”

Nadia Rahman: Technically the metric is `p50_latency_ms / usd` (and also we’ll show p95 separately). Lower is better. It’s basically “milliseconds per USD for a fixed workload definition.”

Henry Cho: Another concern: “latency/$” sounds like a universal truth, but it’s a composite that depends on our pricing + the workload mix. We need the label to signal that.

Jonas Weber: Agree. If we do keep it, we need the tooltip to define it + the methodology link right there.

Wei Chen: Copy idea: call it “Latency per $ (ms/USD)” and add “lower is better” next to it. Or “Cost efficiency (latency per $)” with a learn-more.

Camila Reyes: My gut: we can’t lead with a symbol. “Latency per dollar” is clearer. But we should also test “Performance per dollar” (more common phrase) even if not as precise.

Soojin Lee: Okay, options I’m hearing:
- “Latency/$”
- “Latency per dollar (ms/USD)”
- “Performance per dollar”
- “Cost efficiency”
Anything else?

Ethan Wallace: “Latency-per-dollar” (hyphenated) reads more like a defined term vs a math expression.

Ben Carter: Also “Latency/$” is going to look like a pricing gimmick to some folks on X. “Performance per $” is a known trope.

Nadia Rahman: “Performance per $” is ambiguous though—what performance? tokens/sec? latency? throughput? We’ll still need to explain.

Soojin Lee: Looping Legal before we lock. @Michael @Sofia can you sanity check what’s safest?

Michael Grant: Skimming. Biggest legal risks:
1) People inferring comparative pricing claims (“cheaper”, “lowest cost”, etc.) without full context.
2) Confusion over what “$” represents (your list price vs customer negotiated pricing vs cloud pass-through).
3) “Per dollar” phrasing could be interpreted as a promise of savings.

Sofia Mendes: +1. Also the symbol “$” in a headline can look like marketing-y “value” claims. Not prohibited, but you need guardrails.

Michael Grant: If you use it, define it immediately as a *computed metric* for a *specific workload* under *published assumptions*. Also avoid absolutes (“best”, “#1”, “industry-leading”) unless you’re ready to substantiate across the full market.

Ethan Wallace: Got it. What about “Latency per $ (Redwood pricing)” as a parenthetical?

Sofia Mendes: That helps, but don’t bury it. I’d prefer: “Latency per dollar (ms/USD) — based on Redwood pricing as of <date>” in a footnote + methodology section.

Ben Carter: Footnote is fine in PDF. On the web table we can do a tooltip with “Based on public Redwood API pricing as of July 2025” etc.

Wei Chen: UX-wise: column header could be “Latency per $” and then an info icon. Tooltip: “Computed as p50 end-to-end latency (ms) divided by estimated USD cost for this workload. Lower is better. See methodology.”

Nadia Rahman: + please don’t call it “value index” lol. That sounds like we made it up to win.

Jonas Weber: Also call out that it’s end-to-end latency including queueing under stated concurrency, not just model kernel time. People will try to compare it to their single-request numbers.

Henry Cho: We should avoid “cost efficiency” alone. It’s too broad and invites “you’re saying you’re the cheapest.” If we keep it, it should always be paired with the actual definition.

Camila Reyes: So we can keep the hook but make it defensible:
- Primary label: “Latency per dollar (ms/USD)”
- Short label in charts: “Latency/$ (lower is better)”
- Always include methodology link + pricing date
- Avoid “cheapest” language

Soojin Lee: This feels right. Question: is “per dollar” safer than the “$” symbol?

Sofia Mendes: Slightly. “Per dollar” reads more like a defined unit; “$” reads like a marketing claim. Either is ok if the definition is right next to it.

Michael Grant: Agree. In headlines / hero copy, prefer words over symbols. In tables, symbol is okay with tooltip + footnote.

Ethan Wallace: For the blog headline, what about “latency-per-dollar benchmark” (as a term) and first sentence defines it.

Ben Carter: Works. Also we should add one sentence: “This is not a statement about your total bill; your results vary by prompt mix, context length, caching, and region.”

Nadia Rahman: One more nuance: if we show competitor baselines at all, we can’t imply we know their effective $ unless we specify assumptions. That’s a separate landmine.

Soojin Lee: Yep. We’ll keep competitor context in “contextual baselines” with heavy caveats (and Legal review). This naming decision is mainly for the Redwood numbers.

Wei Chen: Action items:
- I’ll update web spec copy: “Latency per dollar (ms/USD)” + tooltip text + “lower is better” microcopy.
- Ethan: update report glossary entry for “latency per dollar”.

Soojin Lee: I’ll write the approved phrasing + disallowed phrases for the message house.

Michael Grant: Disallowed suggestions (not exhaustive): “cheapest,” “lowest cost,” “save X%,” “best value,” “#1 performance per $,” “guaranteed savings.”

Sofia Mendes: Approved-ish framing: “cost-efficient inference,” “improved latency-per-dollar,” “based on measured workloads,” “results vary.”

Camila Reyes: Perfect. Let’s lock on:
- Hero/headers: “Latency per dollar”
- Chart/table shorthand: “Latency/$” + info tooltip
(reactions: :thumbsup: x6)

Soojin Lee: Locked. I’ll post the exact wording in the enablement docs once Wei/Ethan updates land.

Ben Carter: pls send me the exact tooltip text too so we don’t drift in emails.

Soojin Lee: will do.

title: #marketing: 1749012234
preview:
Soojin Lee: Kicking off the benchmark report workstream here. Goal: align owners + first draft dates + lock a short model/region list we can actually run + publish. 

Soojin Lee: Working doc for now (Drive): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/benchmark-report (outline + calendar placeholders). Please drop edits there but use this thread for decisions.

Camila Reyes: :raised_hands: Thanks Soojin. I want us crisp on “what we measured” and not over-claimy. Also need an internal timeline we can hold people to.

Ethan Wallace: I can own report narrative + blog. Need early inputs: what models, what regions, what workload definition (prefill/decode mix) so I can draft the story arc.

Jonas Weber: Applied ML can own methodology + data quality gates. I’ll pair w/ Claire + Rina. We’ll need a shortlist by end of week to start sweeps + reserve capacity.

Claire Dubois: +1. Also: if we’re going to use “latency/$”, we need to be explicit about pricing basis and how we normalize across context lengths.

Soojin Lee: Proposed owners (sanity check):
- Methodology + harness: Jonas/Claire/Rina
- Report narrative + blog: Ethan
- Launch plan + GTM messaging: me
- Interactive page copy + docs: Wei (loop DevEx)
- Capacity reservations: Vanessa (loop Harish)
- Legal claims review: Michael + Sofia

Soojin Lee: First draft dates (aggressive):
- Outline (v0.3): Fri
- Methodology write-up (v0.1): next Tue
- Data snapshot (first sweep NA only): end of next week
- Report draft (v0.1): 2 weeks from today

Ben Carter: For growth funnel: I can support email + landing CTAs but I need the page URL + what we’re gating vs ungated (PDF?) soon. Also need UTM + events list early.

Wei Chen: I can take landing page / docs work. Need to know: are we calling it “latency per dollar” or something softer? Also disclaimer placement matters.

Camila Reyes: We should keep “latency/$” but add “production inference economics” framing. Let’s not make it sound like a single magic number.

Henry Cho: Product POV: we need a “supported vs experimental” designation for models/regions we publish. I’d prefer we only publish what we can keep stable + offer to customers.

Rina Kobayashi: From harness side, if model list keeps changing mid-run it’s a mess. Please lock shortlist ASAP and then we can add an addendum later.

Soojin Lee: Ok—model list debate. Current shortlist from last meeting:
- Llama 3.1 8B + 70B
- Qwen2.5 7B + 72B
- Mistral Large (if we can) / Mixtral 8x7B
- “One small instruction-tuned” + “one larger general” per family

Jonas Weber: I’d push for: Llama 3.1 8B/70B, Qwen2.5 7B/72B, Mixtral 8x7B, and one “reasoning-ish” model IF it’s stable. But we need to be careful about prompt set compatibility.

Claire Dubois: Mixtral can be noisy in tail latency depending on batching. If we include it, we need to show p95 + throughput alongside the latency/$ composite.

Mateo Alvarez: Runtime note: we can make the results look bad or good depending on KV cache policy and continuous batching knobs. We should pick defaults that match “production recommended” and be transparent.

Vanessa Ortiz: Regions: what are we thinking? I can reserve capacity but I need the exact matrix. Please don’t give me 10 regions x 8 models.

Soojin Lee: Proposed regions for publication:
- NA: us-east, us-west
- EU: eu-west (maybe eu-central depending on availability)
- APAC: apac-sg or apac-tokyo (whichever we can keep consistent)

Vanessa Ortiz: eu-west is usually fine; eu-central is tighter. apac-tokyo is the most constrained. apac-sg tends to be smoother.

Ethan Wallace: For story: “regional consistency” is a key pillar, so at least 1 APAC is important. I’d rather do fewer models than drop APAC.

Henry Cho: Agree, but only if we can say it’s a region we support for real customers (not “best effort”).

Jonas Weber: Suggest compromise:
- Publish: us-east, eu-west, apac-sg
- Internal-only (optional): us-west, eu-central for sanity
Then we can talk about “additional regions available” without publishing claims.

Camila Reyes: That works for me. We can keep charts readable too.

Sofia Mendes: Looping in: please avoid “fastest” / “best in class” language. If we do any comparative statement, it must be scoped + substantiated with retained raw data.

Michael Grant: +1. Also we need a review gate for any competitor mention, even “contextual baselines.” Put a checklist in the process.

Soojin Lee: :noted: So decision proposal (reply with 👍 / concerns):
Models to publish (v1): Llama 3.1 8B/70B, Qwen2.5 7B/72B, Mixtral 8x7B. (Optional 4th TBD after stability check.)
Regions to publish (v1): us-east, eu-west, apac-sg.

Rina Kobayashi: 👍 from me if we lock by EOD Thu so I can wire the sweep runner config and not keep re-cutting manifests.

Mateo Alvarez: 👍. For the optional 4th model: only add if it doesn’t require special-case runtime flags.

Vanessa Ortiz: 👍. For capacity, give me approximate concurrency targets + run windows; I’ll pencil reservations for next week.

Ben Carter: 👍. For landing page, I’ll assume the interactive table starts with those 3 regions and we can add a “more regions” dropdown later once stable.

Wei Chen: 👍. I’ll draft copy with “Regions shown: us-east, eu-west, apac-sg” and link to methodology. Will include a tooltip: “Results vary by workload; see details.”

Ethan Wallace: 👍. If we don’t get a 4th model, I’ll adjust narrative to focus on serving economics + consistency across popular open models.

Claire Dubois: 👍 with one caveat: prompt set needs to include both prefill-heavy and decode-heavy distributions or people will argue it’s cherry-picked. We should preempt that in methodology.

Jonas Weber: +1. We’ll ship prompt set v3 “production mix” and document token length distributions. I’ll post a draft methodology section early next week.

Soojin Lee: Great. Action items:
- Jonas/Claire/Rina: lock workload + prompt set + caching/batching policy in a short “methodology lock” doc by Tue.
- Vanessa: draft capacity reservation plan for us-east/eu-west/apac-sg, with backup if APAC constrained.
- Ethan: start report outline (sections + where charts go) by Fri.
- Wei: landing page copy skeleton + disclaimer blocks by Fri.
- Michael/Sofia: send us the “claims do/don’t” bullets we can paste into message house.

Camila Reyes: Please also add a weekly 30-min check-in for this group. Keep it tight, decisions only.

Noura El-Khoury: I can set up the recurring + start thinking about webinar timing once we have a launch week. Drop preferred dates.

Soojin Lee: I’ll send a calendar hold. Thanks all — keep model/region changes out of band unless it’s a real blocker. :thumbsup:

title: #general: 1747419722
preview:
Ben Carter: PSA: new PLG → Sales signal process (pilot) + what to expect

Starting next week we’re piloting a new “PLG signal routing” workflow to help us catch self-serve accounts that are hitting scale/reliability/compliance needs and *want* to talk — without spamming everyone. Signals will get scored + deduped, then routed into HubSpot as a note + task with an owner.

Pilot scope (Phase 1):
• Region: NA only (initially)
• Cohort: limited set of self-serve orgs above defined usage/intent thresholds
• Motion: human follow-up (no fully automated outbound)

What you might notice:
• Some of you will see new HubSpot tasks titled “PLG Signal Follow-up”
• Company records will get new “PLG Signal” properties + a short signal summary note
• Marketing nurture will be suppressed on those accounts for a short window (to avoid double-touches)

Links (internal):
• PRD: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/PROD/PLG-to-sales-signal-routing-PRD
• Follow-up playbook (cadence + talk tracks): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SE/PLG-signal-follow-up-playbook
• Data handling + retention (privacy review): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/POL/PLG-signal-data-handling-and-retention
• HubSpot properties/workflows spec (Ops checklist): https://drive.google.com/redwoodinference/hubspot-workflows-and-properties-spec

Short FAQ:
1) What counts as a “signal”?
   • Threshold signals: sustained token volume/spend velocity, repeated 429s, multi-workspace expansion
   • Intent signals: security/SOC2 docs views, Dedicated/Private docs interest, VPC/on-prem inquiry

2) Who gets notified?
   • AEs in the NA pilot pool (ownership rules preserve existing owners; otherwise round-robin)
   • CSMs will be tagged on *existing customer* expansions (so we don’t conflict)

3) What data is shared in HubSpot?
   • Aggregated signal summaries + scores (no raw prompts or customer content)
   • See the data handling doc above for exact fields + retention

4) How do I give feedback / report noise?
   • Reply in-thread here, or drop examples in #sales / #marketing / #customer-success
   • If you see a false positive, paste the HubSpot company link + signal note and we’ll tune suppression

Thanks to @Nikhil Sharma, @Stephanie Nguyen, @David Kim, @Tyler Benson, @Isabelle Laurent, @Jasmine Liu + Eng team for pushing this over the line.

:eyes: We’ll share pilot metrics weekly (signal volume, meeting booked rate, false positive rate).

Nikhil Sharma: +1 — goal is “right message at the right time,” not “more outbound.” If you’re seeing tasks that don’t make sense, we want that feedback ASAP.

Stephanie Nguyen: For the NA folks: we’ll do a 20 min enablement walk-through on Monday. I’ll post invite in #sales. The tasks will include the recommended first-touch template + discovery questions.

David Kim: CRM note: you’ll see a new section on Company records: “PLG Signals (last 30d)” + “Last PLG Signal Type”. If anything looks broken (e.g., duplicate tasks), @ me with the record.

Tyler Benson: Marketing side: we’re adding a suppression rule so nurture doesn’t step on AE outreach for ~7 days after a qualified signal. If you think an account should stay in nurture (or be excluded), flag it.

Jasmine Liu: CS heads up: for existing customers, the workflow should tag the CSM + create a CS FYI task (not an SDR task). If you see sales tasks landing on an account with an active CSM-owned expansion plan, please ping me.

Avery Johnson: Question: what’s the expectation on speed to follow up? Same-day?

Ben Carter: @Avery Johnson for the pilot we’re aiming for:
• High urgency (e.g., repeated 429s / latency pain / “Dedicated docs + pricing” intent): same or next business day
• Medium: within 2 business days
Also the task will include an “exit criteria” section so you can close it cleanly if it’s not real.

Markus Klein: From SE side — if the signal is “Private/VPC intent” or “security review content,” please loop an SE early. The playbook has a checklist + who to pull in.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Privacy reminder: please don’t copy/paste customer prompts or outputs into HubSpot notes/Slack when discussing a signal. The pipeline is designed to be aggregate-only; keep follow-up notes high-level.

Camila Reyes: Are we tracking outcomes somewhere consistent? (meeting booked / disqualified / opp created)

Isabelle Laurent: @Camila Reyes yes — in HubSpot we added a required dropdown on the PLG task close: Outcome = {Meeting booked, No response, Disqualified, Existing customer/CS-led, Routed to SE, Other}. That’s what we’ll use for attribution.

Logan Wright: Eng tidbit: aggregation is daily, so signals won’t be “real-time” minute-by-minute. If you see a task, it’s based on sustained activity over a window (helps reduce noise).

Talia Benson: If anyone wants to see the rollout plan + weekly review cadence, it’s in PM-496. We’ll do a weekly 30 min “signal quality” review with Sales/Growth/Ops.

Ben Carter: Thanks all. Keep examples coming — especially the “this would have been helpful” *and* the “this was spammy” ones. That’s how we tune thresholds + suppression.
